basics.name: Yinghang Wang
basics.summary: M.S. candidate in Environmental Sciences at Peking University. Research interests: environmental modeling, air quality, geographic information science, and remote sensing.

location.address: 5 Yiheyuan Road, Yanyuan Subdistrict, Haidian District

work.company: College of Urban and Environmental Sciences, Peking University
work.position: Optimization of a Two-Step Reduced-Form Model for Ambient PM2.5 in China
work.summary: The reduced-form model (RFM) serves as a simplified method to estimate ambient pollutant emissions. PM2.5 concentrations were estimated for three scenarios from 1960 to 2021 using CMAQ.
highlights: The two-step method represents the significance of emission and meteorological factors respectively. The optimal meteorology year was determined under fixed-emission situation.
Regression model was established between PM2.5 concentration and four main affecting pollutant emissions (Primary PM2.5, SO2, NH3, NOx) under fixed-meteorology situation.

Optimization targets spatial discontinuity and negative effects in the regression compared with present RFMs. Generated an initial background PM2.5 concentration baseline as an intercept to resolve spatial discontinuity and applied constrained regression to eliminate the negative impact.

work.position: Updating the Emission Data from 2020 to 2021 of GEMS (Global Emission Modeling System)
work.summary: Familiar with the bottom-up method to compile emission inventory. Emission estimation data was calculated as products of source strength and Emission Factors (EFs), with 225 countries, 9 sectors, 146 sources, and 49 pollutants.
highlights: Estimated EFs and emissions by country, used Monte Carlo simulation to represent uncertainty.
Conducted spatial interpolation of emission data to generate open-source files with 0.1° resolution in both latitude and longitude.

work.position: The Key Technologies of Geological Disaster Dynamic Monitoring Based on Nap-of-the-object Photogrammetry
work.summary: Reconstructed detailed fine 3D real scene model based on multi-period nap-of-the-object photogrammetry. Detected deformation of geological hazards based on 3D model differential analysis.
highlights: Proposed a fast and simple method to locate severely deformed areas caused by collapse and landslide hazards.

Produced high-resolution generalized orthophotos and calculated precise displacement of deformation by feature matching. This work formed the basis of undergraduate thesis and resulted in a conference publication.
